stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \). Compute the directional derivative of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) in the direction of the vector \( \mathbf{v} = \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \). (Use the unit vector in the direction of \( \mathbf{v} \).)
solution:
The gradient is \( 
abla f = \langle 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y,\ 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x angle \), so \( 
abla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) = \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]) + ([[c]])([[x0]]) angle \). The unit vector is \(  rac{1}{[[m]]}\langle [[p]], [[q]] angle \). The directional derivative is \( 
abla f \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \mathbf{v} \) without normalizing it to a unit vector.
This swaps the two components of the direction vector in the dot product.
This adds the gradient components instead of dotting with the unit direction.
This divides by the squared magnitude instead of the magnitude of \( \mathbf{v} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \). Compute the directional derivative of \( f \) at the point \( P = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) in the direction of the point \( Q = ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \) (that is, in the direction of the unit vector from \( P \) toward \( Q \)).
solution: The direction from \( P \) toward \( Q \) is \( \mathbf{v} = Q - P = \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \), with magnitude \( [[h]] \). The gradient at \( P \) is \( \nabla f([[x0]], [[y0]]) = \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]) + ([[c]])([[x0]]) \rangle \), so the directional derivative is \( \nabla f(P) \cdot \frac{\mathbf{v}}{[[h]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the gradient at \( Q \) instead of at \( P \).
This uses \( \mathbf{v} = Q-P \) without normalizing it to a unit vector.
This swaps the two components of \( \mathbf{v} \) in the dot product.
This dots the gradient with the coordinates of \( Q \) itself rather than with \( Q - P \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y, z) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} \). Compute the directional derivative of \( f \) at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]], [[z0]]) \) in the direction of the vector \( \mathbf{v} = \langle [[p]], [[q]], [[s]] \rangle \). (Use the unit vector in the direction of \( \mathbf{v}\).)
solution:
The gradient is \( 
abla f = \langle 2([[a]])x,\ 2([[b]])y,\ 2([[c]])z angle \), so \( 
abla f([[x0]],[[y0]],[[z0]]) = \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[c]])([[z0]]) angle \). The unit vector in the direction of \( \mathbf{v} \) is \( \mathbf{v}/\lVert \mathbf{v} Vert \) with \( \lVert \mathbf{v} Vert = \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}+[[s]]^{2}} \). The directional derivative is \( 
abla f \cdot \hat{\mathbf{u}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \mathbf{v} \) without normalizing it to a unit vector.
This cyclically shifts the components of \( \mathbf{v} \) in the dot product.
This drops the \( z \)-term of the dot product entirely.
This divides by the squared magnitude instead of the magnitude of \( \mathbf{v} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \). The direction \( \mathbf{u} \) makes an angle \( \theta \) with the positive \( x \)-axis where \( \cos\theta = [[cosv]] \) and \( \sin\theta = [[sinv]] \). Compute the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) in the direction \( \mathbf{u} = \langle \cos\theta, \sin\theta \rangle \).
solution: The gradient at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is \( \langle 2([[a]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]])([[y0]]),\ 2([[b]])([[y0]]) + ([[c]])([[x0]]) \rangle \). Since \( \mathbf{u}=\langle\cos\theta,\sin\theta\rangle \) is already a unit vector, the directional derivative is simply the dot product \( \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{u} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps \( \cos\theta \) and \( \sin\theta \) between the two gradient components.
This adds the gradient components instead of dotting with \( \mathbf{u} \).
This drops the \( \sin\theta \) term (the \( y \)-contribution) entirely.
This forgets that \( \langle \cos\theta,\sin\theta\rangle \) is already a unit vector and re-scales by \( u^{2}+v^{2} \) as if it still needed normalizing.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \). Compute the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) in the direction of the vector \( \mathbf{i} = \langle 1, 0 \rangle \) (the positive \( x \)-axis).
solution: Since \( \mathbf{i}=\langle 1,0\rangle \) is already a unit vector along the \( x \)-axis, the directional derivative in this direction equals the partial derivative \( f_{x} \). Here \( f_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), which at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( f_{y} \) (the directional derivative along the \( y \)-axis) instead of \( f_{x} \).
This is the function value \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), not a directional derivative.
This is the rate of steepest ascent (the full gradient magnitude), not the directional derivative along just the \( x \)-axis.
This drops the mixed-term contribution \( [[c]]y \) from \( f_{x} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( f \) is a differentiable function and, at some point \( (a, b) \), the gradient is \( \nabla f(a, b) = \langle [[g1]], [[g2]] \rangle \). What is the directional derivative of \( f \) at \( (a, b) \) in the direction of the vector \( \langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \)?
solution: The directional derivative is the dot product of the gradient with the UNIT vector in the given direction: \( D_{\mathbf{v}}f = \nabla f \cdot \dfrac{\langle [[p]],[[q]]\rangle}{\lVert\langle [[p]],[[q]]\rangle\rVert} = \dfrac{[[g1]]([[p]]) + [[g2]]([[q]])}{[[mag]]} = [[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This uses the direction vector without normalizing it to a unit vector.
This swaps the two components of the direction vector in the dot product.
This divides by the squared magnitude instead of the magnitude itself.
This just adds the two given gradient components, ignoring the direction vector entirely.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{2} + by^{2} \) for an unknown positive coefficient \( b \). At the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), the directional derivative of \( f \) in the direction of the unit vector \( \dfrac{1}{[[mag]]}\langle [[p]], [[q]] \rangle \) equals \( [[d]] \). What is the value of \( b \)?
solution: The directional derivative equation is \( \dfrac{2([[a]])([[x0]])([[p]]) + 2b([[y0]])([[q]])}{[[mag]]} = [[d]] \). Solving for \( b \) gives \( b = \dfrac{[[d]]([[mag]]) - 2([[a]])([[x0]])([[p]])}{2([[y0]])([[q]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has a sign error, adding instead of subtracting the known term before isolating \( b \).
This forgets to divide by \( [[mag]] \) when moving the directional-derivative equation's denominator across.
This drops the known term \( 2ax_0p \) entirely before solving for \( b \).
This forgets to multiply \( [[d]] \) by \( [[mag]] \) before isolating \( b \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
